title: How Palestinians Are Building a Digital Archive That Can’t Be Erased
gist: The Palestinian Museum in Birzeit has built an open-source, geographically distributed 'unlootable' digital archive of over 500,000 digitized Palestinian records, treating replication and backup as a deliberate defense against physical destruction and monthly cyberattacks.

insights.claim: Roughly 80 percent of Palestinian national collections have been looted, destroyed, or remain under Israeli control, according to the Palestinian Museum's general director.
insights.speaker: Tamara Davison
insights.so_what: Quantifies the near-total loss of physical heritage that makes a digital-first preservation strategy existential rather than optional.
insights.quote: roughly 80 percent of the country’s national collections have been looted, destroyed, or remain under Israeli control

insights.claim: Within a week after October 2023, Israeli bombing destroyed two art galleries, seven museums, two main archives, and hundreds of archaeological sites in Gaza.
insights.speaker: Tamara Davison
insights.so_what: Illustrates the speed and scale of institutional destruction that centralized physical archives cannot survive.
insights.quote: Within a week, Israel bombed two art galleries, seven museums, two main archives in Gaza, and hundreds of archaeological sites

insights.claim: As of March 24, 2026, Unesco had verified damage to 164 cultural sites in Gaza since October 7, 2023, spanning historical buildings, religious sites, museums, and archaeological sites.
insights.speaker: Tamara Davison
insights.so_what: Provides an independently verified damage figure that anchors the destruction claims to a third-party institution.
insights.quote: Unesco had verified damage to 164 cultural sites in Gaza since October 7, 2023

insights.claim: A 2025 report by the Applied Research Institute-Jerusalem states at least 2,400 archaeological sites in the West Bank have been taken over by Israel.
insights.speaker: Tamara Davison
insights.so_what: Documents heritage seizure extending beyond Gaza into the West Bank, widening the scope of the preservation threat.
insights.quote: at least 2,400 archeological sites in the West Bank have been taken over by Israel
insights.locator: A 2025 report by the Applied Research Institute-Jerusalem

insights.claim: Israeli lawmakers are advancing legislation to place ancient sites in occupied territory under the Israeli Ministry of Heritage, a move critics call de facto annexation.
insights.speaker: Tamara Davison
insights.so_what: Signals a legal/administrative channel for expanding control over heritage, complementing physical destruction with formal jurisdiction.
insights.quote: a move that Palestinians and Israeli rights groups say amounts to de facto annexation
insights.locator: Reuters reported in June that Israeli lawmakers

insights.claim: The Palestinian Museum began building a digital 'unlootable archive' in 2018, designed to preserve history beyond the walls of any single institution.
insights.speaker: Tamara Davison
insights.so_what: Establishes decentralization as the core design principle for cultural preservation under existential threat, a transferable model for other at-risk communities.
insights.quote: an “unlootable archive”—a digital repository designed to preserve Palestinian history beyond the walls of any single institution

insights.claim: The open-source Palestine Museum Digital Archive contains more than 500,000 digitized photographs, identification papers, diaries, maps, films, and letters, many collected directly from families.
insights.speaker: Tamara Davison
insights.so_what: Demonstrates the scale achievable through grassroots crowd-sourced collection, validating community-sourced digitization as a viable preservation method.
insights.quote: the open-source archive now contains more than 500,000 digitized photographs, identification papers, diaries, maps, films, and letters

insights.claim: A digital archive built bottom-up from family records enables rewriting history from a non-state, distributed base rather than through official state institutions.
insights.speaker: Amer Shomali
insights.so_what: Frames distributed archiving as a means of shifting narrative control away from states, a governance argument relevant to information sovereignty.
insights.quote: it allows you to rewrite the history, but interestingly, bottom-up in the sense that it’s not a state archive
insights.locator: All of a sudden, you start to have this mesh

insights.claim: The project began with door-knocking—visiting West Bank families to scan old photographs, letters, and documents—before scaling into a major digital preservation effort.
insights.speaker: Tamara Davison
insights.so_what: Shows that large digital archives can bootstrap from low-tech, high-trust community outreach rather than institutional collections.
insights.quote: What began with simple door-knocking—visiting families in the West Bank and asking permission to scan old photographs

insights.claim: Multiple copies of the archive are stored around the world in a distributed system, so that if one copy disappears others remain intact.
insights.speaker: Tamara Davison
insights.so_what: Geographic redundancy is the operational backbone of survivability; the same pattern underlies resilient data infrastructure generally.
insights.quote: Multiple copies of the archive are stored around the world, creating a distributed system designed to prevent the collections from disappearing entirely
insights.locator: The museum has also taken steps

insights.claim: The archive's website is subject to cyberattacks almost every month, going down and being reinitiated from one of the maintained backups.
insights.speaker: Amer Shomali
insights.so_what: Quantifies persistent adversarial pressure and shows backup-restore cadence as the practical defense when attacks cannot be prevented.
insights.quote: Almost every month, we get attacked, and the website goes down, and we reinitiate it based on one of the backups we have

insights.claim: The archive's defensive philosophy accepts that the system cannot be protected from being hacked, only from disappearing permanently.
insights.speaker: Amer Shomali
insights.so_what: Reframes security goals from breach-prevention to persistence/availability, a resilience mindset applicable to any adversarial data environment.
insights.quote: We can’t protect it from being hacked, but we can protect it from disappearing.
insights.locator: We can’t protect it from being hacked

insights.claim: The digitization effort runs on just three full-time staff for digitization, metadata, and research, supported by a volunteer network.
insights.speaker: Tamara Davison
insights.so_what: Shows a 500,000-item archive can be sustained by a tiny core team plus volunteers, indicating high leverage per staff member.
insights.quote: a team of three full-time staff members dedicated solely to digitization, metadata, and research
insights.locator: Behind the archive is a team of three

insights.claim: The project is funded through diaspora donations and partnerships with the University of California and the Gerda Henkel Foundation.
insights.speaker: Tamara Davison
insights.so_what: Identifies the funding stack—diaspora plus academic and philanthropic partners—as the sustainability model for stateless cultural preservation.
insights.quote: Funded through diaspora donations and partnerships with the University of California and the Gerda Henkel Foundation

insights.claim: The museum is exploring a bot capable of reading Ottoman Arabic to help process historical records.
insights.speaker: Tamara Davison
insights.so_what: Points to AI/OCR for archaic scripts as an emerging tool that could unlock large volumes of otherwise unreadable historical documents.
insights.quote: The museum is even exploring a bot capable of reading Ottoman Arabic to help process historical records.
insights.locator: The museum is even exploring a bot

insights.claim: An 'exhibition in a box, Ikea-style' initiative lets users download, print, and stage their own Palestine exhibitions anywhere regardless of budget.
insights.speaker: Amer Shomali
insights.so_what: Demonstrates a low-cost, self-service distribution model that multiplies cultural reach without central logistics or capital.

insights.claim: The downloadable exhibition project has been staged more than 260 times from Japan to San Francisco and translated into five languages.
insights.speaker: Tamara Davison
insights.so_what: Quantifies the viral, decentralized reach achievable when preservation output is packaged for self-service replication.
insights.quote: The project has been exhibited more than 260 times, from Japan to San Francisco, and translated into five languages.
insights.locator: The project has been exhibited more than 260 times

insights.claim: The archive has become a source resource for external artists and curators, including a Madrid exhibition that has since traveled to around 15 locations across Spain and drawn Ministry of Culture interest.
insights.speaker: Tamara Davison
insights.so_what: Shows open archives generating downstream cultural production and institutional attention, extending impact beyond the original collection.
insights.quote: the exhibition has travelled to around 15 locations across the country and is attracting interest from Spain’s Ministry of Culture
